guides.title: Rebrand carousel: five jeweled brand icons with one prompt formula
guides.desc: Turn five familiar brand marks — YouTube, X, Quadcode, ChatGPT and Apple — into one luxury object series: glossy inflated metal, crystal detailing, black studio background. One reusable material formula, five swappable silhouettes.

steps.title: 1. Lock the material formula once — then only swap the silhouette
steps.text: The series works because every icon shares one physical recipe: an inflated glossy-metal body, jewel/crystal accents, a pure black studio background and controlled rim lighting. Write that recipe as a fixed block and reuse it verbatim in all five prompts — the only variables are the brand silhouette and its signature color. Icon 01 tests the formula on the YouTube play button: a rounded red jeweled slab with the triangle read as a cut gem.
steps.prompt: Premium 3D brand icon, square 1:1, luxury product photography on a pure black studio background. Subject: the YouTube play-button icon reimagined as a physical jeweled object — a rounded-square slab of glossy inflated red metal, surface like polished automotive lacquer, with the white play triangle inset as a faceted crystal gem catching the light. Fine details: soft balloon-like inflation of the edges, crisp specular highlights, subtle red light bloom on the black floor, micro-scratches absent, showroom-clean. Lighting: one key light from upper left, cool rim light from the right, controlled reflections. Tangible and physical, NOT flat vector art, NOT a UI screenshot. Hard constraints: single object centered, generous negative space, no text, no watermark, no extra logos.

steps.title: 2. X — let black-on-black carry the drama
steps.text: The hardest slide: a black object on a black background. The trick is to spell out the separation mechanism — rim light and crystal facets do all the work. Describe the mark as cut obsidian with jeweled edges so the silhouette reads purely through highlights, not through color contrast.
steps.prompt: Premium 3D brand icon, square 1:1, luxury product photography on a pure black studio background. Subject: the X (Twitter) logomark reimagined as a physical jeweled object — a bold glossy black obsidian X with beveled crystal-cut edges, like a gem carved from dark glass, faint smoky depth inside the material. Separation from the background comes entirely from bright white rim lighting tracing every edge and sharp specular sparkles on the facets. Fine details: inflated rounded body, mirror-grade polish, subtle cool reflection pooled beneath the object. Tangible and physical, NOT flat vector art. Hard constraints: single object centered, generous negative space, no text, no watermark, no extra logos.

steps.title: 3. Quadcode sparkle — the warm focal point of the set
steps.text: Every curated set needs one warm anchor. The Quadcode sparkle takes the brand gradient (#FF9569 → #DD344D) as its actual material color, so it becomes the emotional center of the carousel while still obeying the same formula: inflated gloss, crystal accents, black void around it.
steps.prompt: Premium 3D brand icon, square 1:1, luxury product photography on a pure black studio background. Subject: a four-pointed sparkle/star mark reimagined as a physical jeweled object — glossy inflated metal with a coral-to-crimson gradient finish flowing from #FF9569 at the top points to #DD344D at the base, like anodized liquid metal. A small crystal core glows softly at the center where the four points meet. Fine details: balloon-smooth inflation, razor-clean point tips, warm coral light spill on the black floor, restrained bloom. Lighting: warm key light, cool rim accents. Tangible and physical, NOT flat vector art. Hard constraints: single object centered, generous negative space, no text, no watermark, no extra logos.

steps.title: 4. ChatGPT knot — sell the geometry, not the gloss
steps.text: The knot's interlaced geometry is the whole recognition value, so the prompt protects it explicitly: 'keep every strand separate and readable'. Green gets stated as a material (jade-like metal), not a lighting gel — otherwise the model floods the whole frame green and kills the black background.
steps.prompt: Premium 3D brand icon, square 1:1, luxury product photography on a pure black studio background. Subject: the interlaced hexagonal knot mark reimagined as a physical jeweled object — six interwoven rounded strands of glossy jade-green metal, each strand inflated and balloon-smooth, weaving over and under each other with every strand separate and readable. Crystal-like highlights run along the strand curves; the weave casts soft internal shadows on itself. Fine details: showroom polish, faint green reflection beneath the object, background stays pure black. Lighting: key light from above, thin white rim light tracing the outer strands. Tangible and physical, NOT flat vector art. Hard constraints: single object centered, generous negative space, no text, no watermark, no extra logos.

steps.title: 5. Apple — close the set with restraint
steps.text: The finale goes quiet on purpose: brushed silver, minimal sparkle, maximum surface quality. After four louder objects, restraint is what makes the set read as curated instead of repetitive. Then assemble all five into one 16:10 promo banner — feed the five finished icons back into GPT-Image as references and ask for a single editorial arrangement (that's exactly how the cover of this guide was made).
steps.prompt: Premium 3D brand icon, square 1:1, luxury product photography on a pure black studio background. Subject: the Apple logomark reimagined as a physical jeweled object — glossy inflated silver metal with a soft brushed-chrome finish, the leaf as a separate polished piece with a faint crystal edge. Understated elegance: fewer sparkles than a gem, more mirror-like surface perfection, cool neutral reflections. Fine details: balloon-smooth inflation, immaculate surface, subtle silver glow pooled on the black floor. Lighting: soft key light, crisp white rim light on the silhouette. Tangible and physical, NOT flat vector art. Hard constraints: single object centered, generous negative space, no text, no watermark, no extra logos.

guides.result: Five brand marks rendered as one luxury object family — same inflated glossy-metal material, same black studio, same lighting system, five different silhouettes. Finish by compositing the five icons into a single 16:10 promo banner with GPT-Image using the icons themselves as references. Reuse the formula: keep the material block fixed, swap the silhouette, state the brand color as a material.

guides.title: Direct an epic boss-reveal cinematic shot
guides.desc: One warrior, one colossal tree-golem, a single glowing slash — build a AAA game-trailer beat in 15 seconds flat.

steps.title: 1. Design a boss silhouette you've never seen before
steps.text: Skip generic dragons. A living, cracked tree given a rough humanoid stance instantly reads as ancient and hostile without a single line of backstory — the cherry blossoms softening a threat make it feel like a cursed god of the forest, not a monster-of-the-week.
steps.prompt: A colossal ancient tree given a hulking humanoid silhouette — thick root-legs planted wide, gnarled bark skin split by glowing molten-orange cracks across its chest, branches heavy with pink cherry blossoms, a faint carved face buried in its trunk. Towering scale against a small human figure below. Cinematic realism, volumetric god-rays through blossom petals, dust and pollen drifting in the air, epic AAA game-trailer lighting.

steps.title: 2. Block the shot like a director, not a slideshow
steps.text: One continuous 15-second beat, not three cuts: open low and wide on the boss to sell scale, hold on the lone armored swordsman squaring up beneath it, then push into the exchange. Naming the camera behavior explicitly is what stops the model from defaulting to a static wide shot for the whole clip.
steps.prompt: Cinematic single continuous shot, low wide-angle framing a giant tree-golem boss against cherry blossom trees and a misty stream, warrior in white lamellar armor with twin swords standing small in the foreground. Slow creeping push-in toward the confrontation, natural handheld micro-shake, warm late-afternoon backlight, shallow depth on foreground petals, 15 seconds, no cuts.

steps.title: 3. Give the impact one clear VFX signature
steps.text: A single golden energy arc on the sword slash is the whole payoff of the shot — it needs to read instantly against the dark bark, so keep every other light source warm and secondary. One bright, legible effect beats five competing particle systems.
steps.prompt: The warrior lunges and slashes upward with a katana, leaving one bright golden energy arc trailing the blade that cracks against the tree-golem's molten chest fissure, sparks and bark fragments scattering, camera holds steady through the impact, cherry blossom petals knocked loose drifting past frame, warm rim light, no text, no logos.

steps.title: 4. Cut it, don't trim it
steps.text: Rendered clips run long and often drift in the last second or two — pick the exact in/out points where the wind-up starts and the impact settles, instead of publishing the raw output. Press play above for the final 15-second cut.

guides.result: A 15-second AAA-style boss-reveal cinematic — one creature design, one directed camera move, one clean VFX hit, no cuts needed to sell the scale.

guides.title: Retouch and color grade a product photo into a full ad set
guides.desc: Take one raw headphone photo and turn it into a commercial hero, a clean e-comm shot and a vertical detail — without killing the metal and leather textures.

steps.title: 1. Shoot (or generate) an honest raw source
steps.text: The whole exercise starts from a photo with real-world flaws — flat lighting, fingerprints and fine scratches on the metal, slight wear on the leather. Do not start from a perfect render; you need something to actually retouch. Keep materials detailed and lighting soft so you have room to push the grade later.
steps.prompt: Photorealistic product photograph of premium over-ear headphones on a clean dark studio background. Silver metal ear cups with visible fine scratches, smudges and fingerprints. Black leather ear cushions with natural texture and slight wear. Soft even studio lighting, no dramatic shadows, slightly flat color. Shot on 85mm lens, eye-level, centered composition, clean but imperfect — ready for retouch. High detail on materials, realistic reflections, no text, no logos, no watermark.

steps.title: 2. Retouch and grade the commercial hero
steps.text: This is the controlled edit — not a redesign. You remove scratches and smudges from the metal but keep it brushed and real (no plastic gloss, no chrome mirror). The leather keeps every bit of its grain; you clean dust, not texture. Then you re-light it into a premium hero and apply the cinematic grade. Reframe to 16:9 with breathing room on one side for a future headline.
steps.prompt: Retouch and re-light this exact headphone product photo into a premium commercial hero shot. Keep the same headphones, same materials, same identity — controlled edit only, no redesign. Retouch pass: remove fine scratches, smudges and fingerprints from the silver metal ear cups and yokes. Keep the metal brushed and real — no plastic gloss, no chrome mirror look. Preserve the leather grain on the headband and ear cushions exactly; clean only dust, not texture. Lighting and grade: dramatic premium studio lighting, large soft key from upper-left, cool rim light tracing the metal edges, deep charcoal background with a subtle warm gradient falloff. Rich cinematic color grade: deep blacks, lifted metal highlights, slightly warm silver tone, controlled reflections on the cups — elegant streaks, not chaos. Composition: reframe to a wide 16:9 hero crop, headphones slightly right of center with breathing room on the left for future headline text. Shot on 85mm, shallow depth of field, tack-sharp ear cups. No text, no logos, no watermark, no extra objects, no CGI plastic look.

steps.title: 3. Cut the clean e-comm catalog shot
steps.text: Same retouch rules, totally different light. Marketplaces need honesty: bright even lighting, a neutral seamless background, true-to-life color and the product centered and symmetrical. No stylized grade here — the goal is an accurate, trustworthy listing photo, not a mood piece.
steps.prompt: Retouch this exact headphone product photo into a clean e-commerce catalog shot. Same headphones, same materials, same identity — controlled edit only, no redesign. Retouch pass: remove fine scratches, smudges and fingerprints from the silver metal ear cups and yokes. Keep the metal brushed and real. Preserve leather grain on headband and ear cushions; clean only dust. Lighting and grade: bright, even, neutral catalog lighting — large soft frontal key, gentle fill, no harsh shadows, clean light-grey seamless background (#EDEDED). True-to-life color, no stylized grade: natural silver, deep natural black leather, balanced contrast for marketplaces. Composition: perfectly centered 1:1 square, headphones upright and symmetrical, generous even margins on all sides, product fills ~70% of frame height. Sharp focus across the whole product (f/8 look). No text, no badges, no price tags, no logos, no watermark, no props.

steps.title: 4. Pull the vertical texture detail
steps.text: The 9:16 detail sells the materials. Go macro on the seam where brushed metal meets leather — that tactile contrast is the whole shot. Raking light makes the texture cast micro-shadows. Match the hero's grade so the set reads as one shoot. Texture is the hero here; protect it above everything.
steps.prompt: Create a vertical macro detail shot from this exact headphone product photo. Same headphones, same materials — controlled edit only, no redesign. Subject: extreme close-up of one ear cup where the brushed silver metal meets the black leather cushion — the yoke hinge, the metal ring edge and the leather grain in one frame. Retouch pass: remove scratches, smudges and fingerprints from the metal. Keep the brushed texture and machining lines visible. Preserve leather grain exactly — every pore readable, no smoothing. Lighting and grade: moody macro lighting, raking key light skimming across the metal surface so texture casts micro-shadows, soft fill in the leather, deep dark background falling to black. Rich cinematic grade matching the hero shot: deep blacks, warm-leaning silver highlights, controlled specular streak along the metal edge. Composition: vertical 9:16, macro lens (100mm), shallow depth of field with the metal-leather seam in razor focus. No text, no logos, no watermark, no CGI look, no plastic metal, no blurred-out leather texture.

steps.title: 5. Upscale the whole set to 4K
steps.text: Finish by upscaling every deliverable to 4K. The rule: only add resolution, never reinterpret. A low-strength, quality-first upscaler (SeedVR2 4K) keeps the metal brushed and the leather grain intact instead of smoothing them into plastic. Run the same upscale pass across the set so the resolution jump is consistent.
steps.prompt: Upscale this product photo 2x. Preserve exactly: brushed silver metal texture, black leather grain, the lighting and the color grade. Only add real resolution and crisp micro-detail — do not change lighting, grade, composition or materials. Texture fidelity is everything; no smoothing, no hallucinated detail.

guides.result: A complete, coherent product set from one raw photo: a cinematic 16:9 hero, an honest 1:1 e-comm catalog shot and a vertical 9:16 texture detail — all retouched, graded and upscaled to 4K with the metal and leather textures fully intact.

guides.title: UGC Creator Studio — Build a Consistent AI Influencer & Ship a Reel
guides.desc: Turn one Character Bible into an endless, on-brand content machine: design a persona once, lock her face/tattoos/birthmarks, then auto-generate 10 identity-consistent story frames, stitch them into a vertical Reel, and post — all in one 5-step studio. Every step below shows the exact prompt AND the real result it produces.
guides.models: Seedream v5_pro (identity-preserving frames), Kling (image-to-video), Nanobanana (post mock), agent orchestration (Lumi / Cody / Sonic)

steps.title: 1. Build the persona — one Character Bible, locked forever
steps.text:
Everything starts on the Persona screen. Fill in name, handle, bio, base appearance, build and signature style — but the part that makes this whole system work is the Distinguishing Marks list. You add every birthmark, tattoo and scar with an exact, plain-English location: 'small dark birthmark just below the left corner of her lips', 'fine-line crescent moon on the inner left wrist'. The studio then compiles the entire persona into ONE identity string (the Character Bible) that is silently injected into every image prompt later on. That single string is the reason your creator's face never drifts between shots.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: treat the marks as anchors, not decoration. The more specific and physically-located a mark is ('3 dots behind the right ear', not just 'has freckles'), the more reliably the model reproduces it frame after frame. Write 4–6 marks — enough to lock identity, few enough that the model can honor all of them. The screenshot on the right is the exact Character Bible this guide uses.
steps.prompt: Maya Rivera: Latina woman, 27, warm medium-brown skin, shoulder-length dark wavy hair with subtle caramel balayage, dark brown almond eyes, full brows, natural minimal makeup, softly freckled cheeks. 5 ft 6, slim-athletic build. PERSISTENT DISTINGUISHING MARKS (must be identical in every image): birthmark — small dark birthmark just below the left corner of her lips; birthmark — faint beauty mark above the right eyebrow; tattoo — fine-line crescent moon on the inner left wrist; tattoo — tiny constellation of 3 dots behind the right ear; tattoo — delicate botanical stem along the right forearm; scar — very faint 1cm scar on the left eyebrow. Signature style: warm earthy tones, oversized knits, vintage denim, gold hoop earrings, layered thin necklaces.

steps.title: 2. Generate the reference image — your identity anchor
steps.text:
Press 'Generate reference' and the studio sends the full Character Bible to Seedream as a text-to-image job (v5_pro, portrait). This one photo becomes the anchor for everything downstream — every later frame is edited AGAINST it, so the face and all the marks stay locked. Generate it, zoom in, check that each mark actually rendered (birthmark near the lip, crescent-moon wrist tattoo, botanical forearm tattoo), and regenerate until it truly reads as your creator.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: don't settle on a 'good enough' reference. This image is the source of truth for the entire campaign — a weak or wrong reference will poison all 10 frames. Pick a clean, well-lit, front-facing shot where every mark is clearly visible. The result on the right is Maya's approved anchor.
steps.prompt:
Full-body-to-waist portrait reference sheet of a social-media UGC creator. <CHARACTER BIBLE FROM STEP 1>. Neutral studio background, natural soft lighting, photorealistic, sharp focus, front-facing, clearly showing all distinguishing marks (birthmarks, tattoos). Instagram lifestyle aesthetic. Aspect 3:4.

// Tool: gen_or_edit_image_seedream · VERSION v5_pro · IMAGE_SIZE portrait_4_3 · text-to-image (no input image)

steps.title: 3. Write the scenario — exactly 10 beats, one per frame
steps.text:
A scenario is a template of exactly 10 beats — each beat is a single camera shot. Set the title, the vibe ('cozy, warm light, analog film aesthetic, Lisbon apartment'), the aspect ratio and the post caption. Then either write the 10 beats by hand or hit 'Auto-write 10 beats' to have an agent draft them from the persona + vibe, naturally weaving in her tattoos and birthmark so they actually appear on camera.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: think like an editor cutting a 12-second Reel. Beat 1 is your hook, beats 2–9 carry the micro-story (wake → grind → brew → sip), beat 10 is the payoff/portrait. Keeping it to a strict 10 forces a tight, postable narrative instead of a random photo dump. The screenshot on the right shows the 10 beats fully written.
steps.prompt: Write exactly 10 short visual frame beats (one line each, numbered 1-10) for an Instagram Reels UGC video. Character: Maya Rivera, 27 y.o. lifestyle & coffee creator based in Lisbon, analog photography, cozy cafes, warm and dry-humored. Scenario title: "Slow Morning Coffee Ritual". Vibe: cozy, warm light, analog film aesthetic, Lisbon apartment. Each beat is one camera shot describing pose/action/setting. Occasionally reference her tattoos/birthmark naturally. Return ONLY the 10 numbered lines, nothing else.

steps.title: 4. Generate 10 identity-consistent frames
steps.text:
This is the core trick, and the payoff of everything above. For each frame the studio fuses Character Bible + that frame's beat into one prompt, then calls Seedream in identity-preserving multi-reference mode — passing your reference image as 'Figure 1' so the exact same face, birthmarks and tattoos are carried into every shot. Generate all 10, then run a real editorial loop: keep the good ones, regenerate the misses, approve. When all 10 are approved, the film step unlocks.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: always pass the reference as Figure 1 and explicitly repeat the key marks in the per-frame prompt ('birthmark below the lip, crescent-moon wrist tattoo'). The collage on the right is 4 of Maya's real generated frames side by side — notice the identical face, the same crescent-moon and botanical tattoos, and the consistent film look across completely different poses. That's identity holding across the whole set.
steps.prompt:
Generate an Instagram UGC frame using Seedream identity-preserving multi-reference editing. Use the character reference as IMAGE-INPUT (Figure 1) for identity consistency. Keep the SAME face and ALL distinguishing marks identical to Figure 1 (birthmark below the lip, crescent-moon wrist tattoo, botanical forearm tattoo). <CHARACTER BIBLE>. SCENE: pouring hot water over coffee grounds, steam rising, focused expression. Setting/vibe: cozy, warm light, analog film aesthetic, Lisbon apartment. Photorealistic Instagram UGC photo, cohesive with the reference identity. Aspect 9:16.

// Tool: gen_or_edit_image_seedream · VERSION v5_pro · IMAGE_SIZE portrait_16_9 · reference = Figure 1

steps.title: 5. Assemble the film — 10 frames into a vertical Reel
steps.text:
The 10 approved frames become a moving preview. First you get a live filmstrip inside a phone mockup to check the flow and pacing, then 'Assemble film' renders the real clip: the frames are handed to Kling's image-to-video model, which animates the transitions into a smooth ~12s vertical Reel with subtle motion. Same consistent creator — now in motion.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: preview before you render. The filmstrip is free and instant; the Kling render costs time. Fix beat order and any weak frame first, THEN assemble. The video below is a full screen-recording of the studio flowing through all 5 steps end to end.
steps.prompt: Assemble a short vertical Instagram Reel from these 10 ordered frames (frame_1 … frame_10). Use the Kling image-to-video tool (alias image_to_video_kling) to animate transitions, or stitch into a 9:16 clip (~1.2s per frame) with subtle motion. Keep the creator's identity perfectly consistent across all frames. Save the final vertical video.

steps.title: 6. Publish — see it land in the feed
steps.text:
The final screen is a pixel-accurate Instagram post mock: the creator's avatar and handle, 'Original audio', the Reel preview playing in the media slot, the caption with clickable hashtags, and the like / comment / share row. Hit Publish and it runs an upload animation, likes the post and drops it into a simulated feed — a clean way to preview exactly how the finished piece reads before it goes live for real.

HOW TO APPLY IT YOURSELF: write the caption while the visuals are fresh, and mirror the same vibe words from Step 3 ('slow mornings', 'shot on film'). Front-load the hook, end on a question to drive comments, and keep 3–4 tight hashtags. The result on the right is Maya's finished, ready-to-post Reel — same face and tattoos from Step 2, now sitting in a real feed.
steps.prompt: slow mornings hit different when the light is this good ☕ shot on film, brewed by hand. what's your morning ritual? #slowliving #coffeetok #filmphotography #lisbon

guides.result: One reusable creator you fully own: a locked Character Bible drives a reference photo, ten identity-consistent story frames (same face, same tattoos, same birthmark every time), an auto-assembled vertical Reel, and a ready-to-post Instagram feed mock. Design the persona once — then spin up unlimited on-brand UGC without a camera, a studio, or a real influencer.

guides.title: Quadcode Platform — One Workspace to Build, Ship & Scale
guides.desc: See how a single Quadcode workspace turns one chat request into a full product loop: plan the build, run agents in parallel, wire integrations, and deploy globally — all from one screen.
guides.models: None — Quadcode Platform agents (parallel sub-agent orchestration, live modules, one-click deploy)

steps.title: 1. One workspace, every module in reach
steps.text: The whole product lives on one screen. The right-hand Platform panel is the map: Workspaces (Production / Staging), Projects, Integrations, Deployments, Team, Billing, Analytics and API — each a live module you can open, not a static menu. Instead of stitching five dashboards together, you drive the entire product from a single, coherent surface.

steps.title: 2. Ask in plain language — get a plan, not just an answer
steps.text: You describe the outcome in the chat: 'Scale our platform for the launch — add billing, refresh the dashboard, wire analytics.' The platform doesn't dump a wall of code — it first turns the request into an explicit, ordered plan of tasks, so you can see exactly what will happen before anything runs.
steps.prompt: Scale our Quadcode Platform for launch week: enable metered billing on the Payments module, refresh the Analytics dashboard with real-time charts, connect the analytics data pipeline, and prepare a global one-click deploy. Show me the plan first, then execute.

steps.title: 3. Turn the plan into a task dashboard
steps.text: Each item in the plan becomes a tracked task with its own status: queued, running, done. This is the control tower — at a glance you know what's finished, what's in flight, and what's blocked. No guessing, no hidden background work: every unit of the build is visible and accountable.

steps.title: 4. Run agents in parallel — without them colliding
steps.text: This is the core move: independent tasks fire at the same time on separate sub-agents. Payments scaling, dashboard refresh, analytics migration and an onboarding voiceover all run concurrently, each in its own sandbox with its own status row — spinner while working, checkmark when done. Because the platform isolates their workspaces, parallel work never steps on shared state, so four things ship in the time one used to take.
steps.prompt: Split this into independent tasks and run them in parallel on separate agents: (1) scale Payments module, (2) refresh Analytics dashboard, (3) migrate analytics events, (4) generate onboarding voiceover. Keep each isolated so they don't conflict.

steps.title: 5. Live modules react in real time
steps.text: As the agents work, the platform panel updates itself: services flip to healthy green, the Analytics module fills with fresh charts, Integrations light up as they connect, and the status bar streams progress. You're watching a product assemble itself live — every module reflecting the true, current state of the system rather than a cached screenshot.

steps.title: 6. Wire integrations without leaving the workspace
steps.text: Payments, analytics, storage and auth connect as first-class modules. Instead of copy-pasting keys across separate provider consoles, you approve a connection and the platform provisions it, stores the secret securely, and marks the integration active — keeping the entire wiring step inside one audited flow.
steps.prompt: Connect the Payments and Analytics integrations for this workspace, store the credentials securely as workspace secrets, and mark them active. Don't expose keys in code.

steps.title: 7. One-click global deploy
steps.text: When the tasks land, deployment is a single action. The globe module lights its edge nodes across regions and the Deployments module goes live — the same workspace that planned and built the release also ships it worldwide, with rollout status visible the whole way. No handoff, no separate CI dashboard.
steps.prompt: Deploy the updated workspace to production across all edge regions with one click, run health checks, and report the rollout status per region.

steps.title: 8. The full loop, in one place
steps.text: Plan → parallel build → live modules → integrations → global deploy — the entire product lifecycle happened on a single screen, driven by one conversation. That's the point of the platform: not a faster editor, but one unified workspace where an idea becomes a shipped, running, scaling product without ever leaving it.

guides.result: A single Quadcode workspace that takes one plain-language request and drives the whole loop — an ordered plan, a live task dashboard, four agents running in parallel without conflict, self-updating platform modules, securely wired integrations, and a one-click global deploy. One screen, one conversation, a shipped and scaling product.

guides.title: Turn business problems into visuals people understand
guides.desc: Make four clear business visuals: recurring revenue, AI onboarding, faster landing pages and a CRM that replaces spreadsheet chaos.

steps.title: 1. Show recurring revenue as a system
steps.text: A revenue visual should make the mechanism visible in one glance: payment happens, the service continues, the customer returns. Avoid vague arrows and fake dashboard numbers.
steps.prompt: Create a clear premium business illustration explaining recurring revenue. Show a simple continuous loop between customer, payment and active service, with a readable 24/7 operating rhythm. Use a clean editorial SaaS style, strong hierarchy, restrained palette, real interface fragments rather than generic arrows, no fake statistics, no illegible microtext.

steps.title: 2. Contrast manual onboarding with AI onboarding
steps.text: The visual needs one contrast only: a slow hand-built process on one side, an automated flow on the other. People should understand the time saving before reading a caption.
steps.prompt: Create a split business visual comparing manual onboarding versus AI onboarding. Left: fragmented handoffs, repeated data entry, waiting and confusion. Right: one guided automated flow, instant setup and clear next steps. Professional editorial SaaS art direction, readable contrast, few elements, no decorative clutter, no fake logos.

steps.title: 3. Make a landing page's speed visible
steps.text: Do not render a generic browser mockup. Show the commercial consequence: a page that takes a week to ship versus a focused flow that gets the message and call-to-action live fast.
steps.prompt: Create a crisp business visual about shipping a landing page in one week. Show a focused sequence from brief to launch: message, page structure, build and live result. Make speed readable through progress and momentum, not flames or racing cars. Premium product-design editorial style, clear hierarchy, no fake analytics, no tiny unreadable text.

steps.title: 4. Replace spreadsheet chaos with a CRM view
steps.text: The before-and-after must be practical: scattered rows and lost follow-ups become one shared pipeline with owners, stages and next actions. That is what a CRM actually fixes.
steps.prompt: Create a business before-and-after visual: an overloaded spreadsheet with duplicate customer rows, unclear ownership and missed follow-ups transforms into a clean CRM pipeline with visible stages, owner avatars and next actions. Product-led editorial design, strong information hierarchy, realistic but minimal interface detail, no brand names, no illegible text.

guides.result: Four business visuals that explain a real operational change in seconds — useful for landing pages, investor decks, sales posts and internal presentations.

guides.title: Compare three interior directions before you commit
guides.desc: Use the same real-estate brief to compare a Baroque cottage, an industrial loft and a modern studio — then choose a direction with a clear point of view.

steps.title: 1. Lock what must stay the same
steps.text: Start with one comparable brief: room size, daylight direction, camera height and the buyer profile. If those change between renders, you are comparing different apartments rather than different interior directions.
steps.prompt: Create three high-end real-estate interior concepts for the exact same compact urban living room. Keep the architecture, camera angle, daylight direction and room proportions identical in every version. The comparison must show only the interior direction changing: furniture, material palette, lighting mood and decorative language. Editorial property-photography quality, realistic scale, no people, no text.

steps.title: 2. Push the Baroque cottage direction
steps.text: For a character-led property, use dark timber, aged brass, velvet and framed art. The goal is not random luxury props; it is a warm, layered room that sells atmosphere at first glance.
steps.prompt: Using the locked room brief, create the Baroque cottage direction: dark walnut joinery, muted olive walls, aged brass details, a deep plum velvet sofa, antique framed oil paintings, patterned wool rug, warm late-afternoon window light and restrained candle-like practical lamps. Rich but lived-in, high-end property editorial photography, no text.

steps.title: 3. Switch to an industrial loft
steps.text: For a sharper, city-facing listing, replace decoration with material contrast: concrete, black steel, reclaimed wood and one strong leather piece. Keep the room functional — buyers still need to see where they would sit, work and eat.
steps.prompt: Using the locked room brief, create the industrial loft direction: exposed concrete, black steel-framed glass, reclaimed oak, a cognac leather sofa, graphite textiles, sculptural pendant lights and clear daylight. Keep the space warm enough to live in, not like an empty warehouse. Premium real-estate editorial photography, no text.

steps.title: 4. Strip it back into a modern studio
steps.text: For the broadest buyer appeal, use a quiet palette and fewer objects. A modern studio succeeds when the layout reads instantly and every piece earns its place.
steps.prompt: Using the locked room brief, create the modern studio direction: warm white walls, pale oak, a modular cream sofa, brushed aluminium accents, one large abstract artwork, soft indirect lighting and discreet storage. Calm, spacious, design-led and practical. Premium real-estate editorial photography, no text.

guides.result: Three coherent directions for the same property, ready to show a client or test in a listing: character-rich Baroque cottage, urban industrial loft and calm modern studio.

guides.title: Upscale real product footage to true 4K
guides.desc: Turn a soft handheld watch shoot into a crisp 4K before/after with one Topaz pass.

steps.title: Match the model to your footage
steps.text: Real product photography isn't heavily compressed or synthetic, so skip the specialty models and start with the general-purpose one — it sharpens detail without over-smoothing metal reflections and skin-tone gemstones into plastic.
steps.prompt:
UTILITY: topaz
VIDEO-INPUT: rolex-source.mp4
MODEL: Proteus
TARGET_FPS: 30

steps.title: Keep the upscale factor and noise conservative
steps.text: For footage that's already reasonably sharp, 2x beats 4x — pushing harder just amplifies grain instead of adding real detail. Keep noise and compression in the 0.1-0.3 range so the case metal and dial texture stay crisp.
steps.prompt:
UTILITY: topaz
VIDEO-INPUT: rolex-source.mp4
MODEL: Proteus
UPSCALE_FACTOR: 2
NOISE: 0.2
COMPRESSION: 0.2
H264_OUTPUT: true

steps.title: Render and cut the before/after
steps.text: Run the pass, then cut between the original and the upscaled clip on the same shot so the sharpness gain is obvious at a glance, not just claimed in a caption. Play the clip above to see the result.

guides.result: A real handheld watch shoot turned into a crisp, true-4K before/after comparison — one Topaz pass, no reshoot needed.

guides.title: Pitch a flying ironing-drone concept ad
guides.desc: Turn a wild what-if into a punchy product-ad concept: a hovering drone that steam-irons your shirt mid-air.

steps.title: Invent the product in one line
steps.text: Name the object and the twist in a single sentence — a compact drone with a heated glide bar is a small enough leap to feel plausible, but strange enough to stop the scroll.
steps.prompt: A sleek white quadcopter drone about the size of a dinner plate hovers beside a hanging crisp white dress shirt on a wooden hanger, a thin heated glide bar mounted under its body gliding down the fabric like a flatiron, wisps of steam rising from the contact point, dark charcoal studio background, dramatic side lighting, moody premium product-ad photography, no text.

steps.title: Direct the ironing pass and the captions
steps.text: Script it like a real spec ad: name each beat and give the exact caption text for each one, so the render stays on-message instead of drifting into generic footage.
steps.prompt: The drone glides slowly down each panel of the shirt, steam trailing behind its heated bar, wrinkles visibly smoothing wherever it passes. Cut between beats with these on-screen captions, small caps, bottom-center, clean sans-serif: "THIS IS IRONDRONE" then "GLIDING STEAM" then "LASER PRECISION" then "EVERY CREASE — ERASED, MID-AIR" then "CRUMPLED — TO FLAWLESS". End on the shirt hanging perfectly flat and crease-free. Same dark studio, same lighting throughout, no extra text.

steps.title: Generate and keep the cleanest pass
steps.text: Run it and pick the take where the steam reads as heat, not fog, and the crease actually disappears on camera — that's the shot that sells the fake product. Press play above to see the result.

guides.result: A 10-second concept ad for a product that doesn't exist yet — proof that a strong enough "what if" beats a real product shoot.

guides.title: Shoot a macro vinyl-groove banner
guides.desc: Turn one macro record shot into a clean cover image and a branded 16:10 promo banner.

steps.title: Shoot the macro hero
steps.text: Describe the object, not a mood word. A spinning record with a tonearm lowering onto the groove reads as "music" instantly — no abstract metaphor needed.
steps.prompt: Extreme macro studio photograph, wide 3:2 landscape composition, shot on a medium-format camera with a 100mm macro lens, razor-shallow depth of field. A spinning black vinyl record fills most of the frame at a low raking angle so the concentric grooves catch the light. A chrome tonearm with a diamond stylus enters from the upper right, tip lowering onto the groove, caught mid-motion with a faint motion blur. Warm amber-gold key light raking low from the left, cool blue-white rim light on the tonearm's chrome edge, deep black background, rich analog film grain, warm cinematic color grade. No text, no logos, no people.

steps.title: Recompose into a branded banner
steps.text: Feed the hero shot back into GPT-Image and ask it to extend the frame to 16:10 with a caption zone on the dark side. Naming exact colors and the exact title text keeps the typography clean and on-brand.
steps.prompt: Recompose this photo to fill a full 16:10 landscape frame, keeping the vinyl record, tonearm and lighting exactly as in the reference. Outpaint the black background on the right into calm dark negative space, then add a caption there: a coral-to-red gradient accent bar, bold condensed all-caps title "MUSIC", and a smaller subtitle "Turn one prompt into a track." in white and light gray, with a thin corner-bracket frame around the caption zone. Near-black navy background, not pure black. No watermark, no extra text.

guides.result: A clean cover shot plus a branded 16:10 promo banner for the Music category, built from one macro photo.

guides.title: Museum of Impossible Things: a 5-image carousel with one prompt formula
guides.desc: Five physically impossible exhibits shot like real museum photography — a water knot, frozen lightning, fire inside ice, a storm in a bottle and a folded human shadow. One reusable scene formula, five swappable subjects, readable plaques on every frame.

steps.title: 1. Lock the scene formula once — then only swap the exhibit
steps.text: The whole series rides on one fixed skeleton: a pitch-dark museum hall with black velvet walls, one hard spotlight, a charcoal velvet pedestal, and a graphite plaque with a gradient accent strip. Every prompt starts with the same camera line — 'Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1, medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, extremely fine micro-detail' — and ends with the same bans: no CGI plastic look, no stock blandness, no extra text. The only thing that changes between the five images is the impossible object on the pedestal and the plaque text. That's what makes five separate generations read as one exhibition. Exhibit 01 tests the formula: a trefoil knot tied from flowing water, hovering above the velvet with caustic light patterns underneath.
steps.prompt: Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1 composition, shot on medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, razor-sharp focus on the exhibit, extremely fine micro-detail. Scene: a pitch-dark museum hall, walls of black velvet swallowing all light. One single hard spotlight beam cuts down through faint dust. Hero subject, filling most of the frame: a thick rope tied into a perfect trefoil knot — made entirely of living, flowing crystal-clear water. Visible surface tension skin, internal turbulence, strings of micro-bubbles racing through the loops, dozens of individual droplets frozen mid-orbit around the knot, each droplet acting as a tiny lens. The knot hovers above a round pedestal upholstered in deep charcoal velvet — velvet fiber texture clearly visible. Sharp bright caustic light patterns dance across the velvet under the knot. Color and light: deep glacial blue-white water against warm amber spill on the velvet, saturated punchy cinematic grade, crushed blacks, high dynamic range. Tangible and physical — a photograph of a real impossible object, NOT a 3D render, NOT smooth CGI plastic. In the lower portion of the frame: a museum plaque — matte graphite metal plate with a thin glowing gradient strip along its top edge running from coral orange (#FF9569) to crimson red (#DD344D). Plaque text painted white, upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic), perfectly sharp and legible, EXACTLY verbatim: "WATER KNOT" / "Exhibit No. 01 — Fluid Dynamics Hall" / "Tied once. Never untied. The water has been flowing through itself since the day it was knotted." Hard constraints: no cyberpunk, no neon, no sci-fi props, no people, no watermarks, no logos, no extra text, no plastic CGI look.

steps.title: 2. Frozen Lightning — make the impossible object the only light source
steps.text: The trick that sells this frame: the exhibit itself lights the scene. The bolt is described as white-hot plasma (not a purple neon tube — ban that explicitly, or you'll get a lamp), and physicality comes from consequences: a scorched blackened ring on the velvet where the bolt touches it, glowing embers at its rim, a wisp of smoke. If your plaque text comes out italic, add 'upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic)' — that one line fixed it here.
steps.prompt: Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1 composition, shot on medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, razor-sharp focus on the exhibit, extremely fine micro-detail. Scene: a pitch-dark museum hall, walls of black velvet swallowing all light. Hero subject, filling most of the frame: a real lightning bolt frozen solid mid-strike, displayed upright like a sculpture on a round pedestal upholstered in deep charcoal velvet (velvet fiber texture clearly visible). The bolt is a jagged branching column of blinding white-hot plasma, completely motionless — every filament crisp and separate, hair-thin secondary tendrils splitting off the main channel, dozens of tiny sparks suspended motionless in the air around it like insects trapped in amber. Where the bolt meets the velvet, the fabric is scorched into a delicate blackened ring with glowing orange embers at its rim, a thin wisp of smoke rising. Faint crackled glassy texture on the plasma surface, heat shimmer distortion in the air around it. Color and light: the bolt is the only light source — blinding white core with violet-blue falloff, hard shadows, crushed blacks, saturated punchy cinematic grade, high dynamic range. NOT a 3D render, NOT smooth CGI plastic, NOT a purple neon lamp. In the lower portion of the frame: a museum plaque — matte graphite metal plate with a thin glowing gradient strip along its top edge running from coral orange (#FF9569) to crimson red (#DD344D). Plaque text painted white, upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic), perfectly sharp and legible, EXACTLY verbatim: "FROZEN LIGHTNING" / "Exhibit No. 02 — Storm Preservation Wing" / "Captured 0.003 seconds before impact. Still warm. Do not tap the glass." Hard constraints: no cyberpunk, no neon city, no sci-fi props, no people, no watermarks, no logos, no extra text.

steps.title: 3. Ice with Fire — the color-clash hero of the series
steps.text: This is the loudest slide, built on one violent contrast: blazing orange fire core versus glacial blue-cyan ice shell. Push both to maximum saturation and let the fire light travel through internal fractures so every crack becomes a glowing amber vein. Physicality anchors that keep it from looking CGI: frost bloom, cold droplets sweating down the faces, trapped air bubbles in ancient ice layers, and a glassy refrozen rim of meltwater on the velvet below.
steps.prompt: Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1 composition, shot on medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, razor-sharp focus on the exhibit, extremely fine micro-detail. Scene: a pitch-dark museum hall, walls of black velvet swallowing all light. One single hard spotlight beam from above. Hero subject, filling most of the frame: a massive raw block of glacier ice on a round pedestal upholstered in deep charcoal velvet (velvet fiber texture clearly visible). Deep inside the crystal-clear ice a real campfire burns — sharp orange-gold flames with visible flame licks, glowing red embers and charred logs at its base, all fully sealed within the ice. The fire light travels through internal fractures, turning every crack into a glowing amber vein across the block. The ice exterior is hyper-detailed: frost bloom, sweating cold droplets running down the faces, tiny trapped air bubbles in ancient layers, razor-sharp chipped edges. Thin freezing mist rolls off the block and pools around the pedestal base. Below the block, melted water refreezes into a glassy rim on the velvet. Color and light: violent clash — blazing orange fire core versus deep glacial blue-cyan ice shell, saturated punchy cinematic grade, crushed blacks, high dynamic range. NOT a 3D render, NOT smooth CGI plastic. In the lower portion of the frame: a museum plaque — matte graphite metal plate with a thin glowing gradient strip along its top edge running from coral orange (#FF9569) to crimson red (#DD344D). Plaque text painted white, upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic), perfectly sharp and legible, EXACTLY verbatim: "ICE WITH FIRE" / "Exhibit No. 03 — Thermodynamics Denial Hall" / "The fire refuses to melt it. The ice refuses to put it out. They have agreed to disagree." Hard constraints: no cyberpunk, no neon, no sci-fi props, no people, no watermarks, no logos, no extra text.

steps.title: 4. Storm in a Bottle — change the scale, keep the system
steps.text: Four monumental exhibits in a row get monotonous. Slide four drops to macro scale: a 40 cm antique bottle with a full thunderstorm inside. Ask for 'macro-level sharpness' instead of the standard focus line and load the prompt with tiny believable details — condensation streaks inside the glass, bubbles in the hand-blown glass itself, cracking wax on the cork, one wisp of cloud escaping the seal. The internal lightning flash does the lighting job again, carving the bottle silhouette out of the darkness.
steps.prompt: Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1 composition, shot on medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, macro-level sharpness on the exhibit, extremely fine micro-detail. Scene: a pitch-dark museum hall, walls of black velvet swallowing all light. One single hard spotlight beam from above. Hero subject, filling most of the frame: an antique hand-blown glass bottle with a waxed cork, standing on a round pedestal upholstered in deep charcoal velvet (velvet fiber texture clearly visible). Inside the bottle rages a full miniature thunderstorm: dense churning storm clouds pressed flat against the inner glass, a brilliant forked lightning bolt flashing between cloud layers, sheets of miniature rain hammering a furious micro-ocean with breaking whitecap waves at the bottom. Condensation droplets and rain streaks running down the inside of the glass, salt-spray fog above the micro-waves, imperfections and bubbles in the antique glass itself, wax cracking on the cork, one thin escaping wisp of storm cloud curling out past the cork seal. The lightning flash inside throws stuttered blue-white light onto the velvet and carves the bottle silhouette out of the darkness. Color and light: cold electric blue-white storm light against the warm dark amber hall, saturated punchy cinematic grade, crushed blacks, high dynamic range. NOT a 3D render, NOT smooth CGI plastic. In the lower portion of the frame: a museum plaque — matte graphite metal plate with a thin glowing gradient strip along its top edge running from coral orange (#FF9569) to crimson red (#DD344D). Plaque text painted white, upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic), perfectly sharp and legible, EXACTLY verbatim: "STORM IN A BOTTLE" / "Exhibit No. 04 — Contained Weather Collection" / "Category 5, corked in 1883. Uncorking voids the museum's insurance." Hard constraints: no cyberpunk, no neon, no sci-fi props, no people, no watermarks, no logos, no extra text.

steps.title: 5. Folded Human Shadow — close on the concept, not the spectacle
steps.text: The finale trades fireworks for a paradox: a human shadow folded like boutique silk, lying inside a bright spotlight yet staying absolute black. The prompt spells the contradiction out — 'it lies directly inside the bright spotlight yet remains total, light-devouring black, zero reflection'. Recognizability sells it: a face profile on the top layer, a folded arm, shadow fingers hanging over the edge. Two extra touches give it life: one corner curling up as if about to unfold, and the velvet subtly darker around the stack, as if the shadow stains what it touches. Plaque copy carries the storytelling — write it like a real museum label with one unsettling line.
steps.prompt: Award-winning museum exhibition photograph, square 1:1 composition, shot on medium-format Hasselblad, 80mm lens, razor-sharp focus on the exhibit, extremely fine micro-detail. Scene: a pitch-dark museum hall, walls of black velvet swallowing all light. One single hard warm spotlight beam from above with faint dust drifting through it. Hero subject, filling most of the frame: a full-size human shadow — a flat, absolutely black silhouette of a standing person — carefully folded like fine silk fabric into a neat boutique-style stack, displayed on a round pedestal upholstered in deep charcoal velvet (velvet fiber texture clearly visible). The folded stack is instantly readable: the profile of a face on the top layer, a folded arm tucked into the second layer, slender shadow fingers spilling over the edge of the fold and hanging in the air. The shadow material is impossible: it lies directly inside the bright spotlight yet remains total, light-devouring black — zero reflection, deeper black than anything else in the frame. Its edges are soft and feathered exactly like a real cast shadow, yet it holds crisp fabric creases; one corner curls up slightly as if the shadow is about to unfold itself. Around the stack the velvet is subtly darker, as if the shadow slowly stains what it touches. Color and light: warm amber spotlight against charcoal darkness, the folded shadow reading as an absolute black hole in the image, saturated punchy cinematic grade, crushed blacks, high dynamic range. NOT a 3D render, NOT smooth CGI plastic. In the lower portion of the frame: a museum plaque — matte graphite metal plate with a thin glowing gradient strip along its top edge running from coral orange (#FF9569) to crimson red (#DD344D). Plaque text painted white, upright regular sans-serif (NOT italic), perfectly sharp and legible, EXACTLY verbatim: "FOLDED HUMAN SHADOW" / "Exhibit No. 05 — Department of Lost Properties" / "Donor unknown. Folded, never claimed. Please do not stand too close — it remembers how to follow." Hard constraints: no cyberpunk, no neon, no sci-fi props, no people, no watermarks, no logos, no extra text.

guides.result: A five-image carousel that reads as one exhibition: same hall, same velvet pedestal, same branded plaque system (graphite plate, coral-to-crimson gradient strip, readable sans-serif labels) — with five different impossible objects. All frames generated square 2880x2880 in GPT-Image, no upscaling needed. Reuse the formula: keep the scene skeleton, swap the exhibit, rewrite the plaque.

guides.title: FlowForge: a monochrome workflow-diagram editor, built from scratch
guides.desc: A full black-and-white SaaS build: animated landing page, node-based diagram editor, a systemized logo/brand kit, and pixel-exact favicon/OG assets, all vanilla JS with zero dependencies.
guides.models: Nanobanana (brand illustrations), HTML/canvas render (favicon & OG rasterization)

steps.title: 1. Lock the design tokens before anything else
steps.text: Start with a single tokens.css file: a strict two-color palette (#0a0a0a ink / #ffffff paper, plus muted greys, no color accent anywhere), Inter for UI type and JetBrains Mono for code/labels, and named easing curves (--ease-out, --ease-in-out, --ease-spring) with duration tokens instead of magic numbers scattered across files. This single source of truth is what keeps every later screen, illustration and animation feeling like one product. Also bake in prefers-reduced-motion support globally from day one.

steps.title: 2. Build the hero with a live animated diagram, not a static screenshot
steps.text: The hero's centerpiece is a real inline SVG workflow (Start -> Send welcome / Verify email -> Active? -> Done) with CSS-driven entrance animation and a traveling pulse dot that runs along the actual connector path (offset-path), so the very first thing a visitor sees is the product's core interaction, not a mockup. Nodes fade/scale in with staggered delays; the pulse loops continuously at a slow, calm pace to match the monochrome, unhurried brand tone.

steps.title: 3. Ship the feature grid with a real editorial art band
steps.text: Instead of another row of flat icon cards, each major section (Features, Logic) opens with a full-bleed black-and-white editorial illustration, generated to match the brand's exact visual language: fine halftone grain, raking studio light, one small open-node/filled-node motif echoing the logomark. This turns a generic SaaS feature list into something with an actual point of view. Underneath, six feature cards cover node canvas, bezier links, logic templates, export, autosave and keyboard-first controls.
steps.prompt: Editorial black-and-white illustration, precision engineering close-up: clean geometric mechanical parts (gears, pins, calipers) mid-assembly, fine halftone/print-grain texture, strong raking studio light, deep blacks, no color anywhere, generous negative space for text overlay.

steps.title: 4. Build the node-based canvas: drag, drop, connect
steps.text: The editor (app.html) has a real pan/zoom canvas with six node types (start, action, condition, loop, parallel, end), each dragged in from a left palette. Edges are smooth SVG bezier curves drawn live while connecting two ports, including backward-loop routing so retry/rollback arrows curve around instead of overlapping nodes. Node position lives on a transform-only wrapper (GPU-accelerated), decoupled from the node's own entrance-scale animation, so dragging never fights the mount animation.

steps.title: 5. Ship four ready-made logic templates, not a blank canvas
steps.text: A blank canvas is intimidating. FlowForge ships four one-click templates that cover the four core branching patterns any workflow tool needs to demonstrate: Approval chain (sequential sign-off with escalation branches), CI/CD pipeline (linear stages with a retry loop), User onboarding (parallel fan-out) and ETL / data flow (merge + quality-gate routing). Each template card on the landing page previews its own miniature diagram so the pattern is recognizable before you even open the editor.
steps.prompt: Editorial black-and-white illustration, architectural branching motif: thin rigid lines diverging from one origin point and reconverging, like a railway switchyard or split optical fibers, fine halftone grain, strong directional light, no color.

steps.title: 6. Close with pricing and a brand-illustration CTA band
steps.text: Pricing stays true to the 'two colors, three plans' promise: a free Sketch tier, a Studio team tier, and a Forge enterprise tier, no fourth color, no exceptions. The final CTA band uses the same node/edge editorial illustration as a real background image (dark gradient overlay for text contrast) instead of a flat solid block, so even the last screen before conversion reinforces the brand world rather than defaulting to a plain color rectangle.

steps.title: 7. Systemize the logo into a real favicon + OG asset pipeline
steps.text: The logomark (rounded tile, one open node, one filled node, one diagonal edge) is defined once as an exact SVG and never redrawn or reinterpreted. Favicons (32px, 192px, 180px apple-touch-icon) and the 1200x630 Open Graph banner are produced via deterministic HTML/canvas rendering of that same SVG, not AI regeneration, so the tab icon and the social-share card are pixel-identical to the logo in the navbar. Gotcha worth documenting: rendering directly at very small canvas sizes (32-192px) corrupted the output; rendering at 512x512 first and downscaling fixed it completely. A full brandbook overview board and a standalone editorial illustration round out the identity kit.
steps.prompt: Premium brand guidelines overview board, 3x3 grid: logomark construction, digital app usage, brand essence, color system (strict monochrome), typography, physical/print application, image direction, UI system. Black-and-white, gallery-quality, no color accents.

guides.result: A complete, deployable monochrome SaaS: an animated marketing site, a working node-based diagram editor with JSON/SVG export and localStorage autosave, a systemized logo/brand kit, and a pixel-exact favicon/OG pipeline, all in vanilla HTML/CSS/JS with no build step and no dependencies.

guides.title: Screenshot to Pixel-Perfect UI — Now with a Premium Redesign
guides.desc: Hand the agent one UI screenshot — get back real, responsive, interactive code. Then push it further: living animated cover art, full playback states, draggable waveform, and a premium glass-and-gold visual pass.
guides.models: None — Quadcode.ai IDE agent (canvas pixel-sampling + browser screenshot verification loop)

steps.title: 1. Start from one screenshot — that's the whole spec
steps.text: No Figma file, no design tokens. Just a single UI screenshot of a 'Now Playing' music widget. The image itself becomes the style guide: every color, radius and gap gets pulled straight from its pixels instead of guessed.

steps.title: 2. Measure the reference, don't eyeball it
steps.text: Load the screenshot into a browser <canvas> and sample real pixel data: background vs card-surface color, exact border-radius by scanning where colors change, content padding, and the accent gradient's hex values. That turns 'looks about right' into numbers you can actually build from.

steps.title: 3. Build the base component — HTML/CSS + minimal JS
steps.text: Recreate the card as one responsive file: flexbox layout, CSS gradients for the accent color and album art, clamp() for fluid sizing, plus vanilla JS for the interactive bits. No framework, no build step — verified with a side-by-side against the source until proportions matched exactly.

steps.title: 4. Round 2: a living album cover, not a static swatch
steps.text: Swapped the flat gradient square for a layered composition — drifting radial gradients that slowly shift and breathe, plus a soft diagonal shine sweep that passes over the art every few seconds. It reacts to state: motion pauses the instant playback pauses, no separate toggle needed.

steps.title: 5. Real playback states, not just play/pause
steps.text: Added a proper state machine: idle → loading → playing → paused. Switching tracks (via Next/Prev or the queue) triggers a short loading state — spinner over the play button, title/artist cross-fade, cover art swaps gradient — before the new track starts, instead of an instant unrealistic cut.

steps.title: 6. Draggable waveform progress + smart volume
steps.text: Replaced the plain progress line with a 46-bar waveform (two identical bar sets layered — one gray, one gradient-filled and width-clipped to the progress %). Fully draggable with a live time tooltip, and the timer auto-advances every second while playing. Volume follows the same drag pattern, with a mute button that remembers your last level and swaps its icon between muted/low/high.

steps.title: 7. Like button with a real micro-interaction
steps.text: Tapping like doesn't just flip a color — it pops the button (scale bounce) and bursts 8 tiny heart/spark particles outward that fade over ~0.85s, generated and cleaned up on the fly with no extra assets.

steps.title: 8. Shuffle, repeat, and an 'Up Next' queue
steps.text: Added shuffle and repeat toggles (gold accent + dot indicator when active) and a scrollable queue below the player showing the next tracks with mini cover swatches — click any of them to jump straight to that track, triggering the same loading transition.

steps.title: 9. The premium visual pass
steps.text: Final polish layer: a slowly rotating conic-gradient border (purple → blue → gold) around the whole card, a gold 'PREMIUM' badge and 'Lossless' chip, an animated equalizer indicator, Fraunces serif italic for the track title, blurred ambient color blobs drifting behind the card, a subtle film-grain overlay, and a soft 3D tilt that follows the cursor. Same layout and logic underneath — just dressed up.

guides.result: A fully responsive, interactive premium music player component: animated cover art, real loading/playing/paused states, draggable waveform + volume, shuffle/repeat, an up-next queue, and a rotating gold-and-gradient visual finish — one file, zero dependencies.

guides.title: Make a liquid AI "essence" orb (Siri/Gemini-style)
guides.desc: Create a glossy purple liquid-metal blob that morphs and flows in place — the kind of living AI voice-orb you see in ChatGPT and Yandex Music.

steps.title: 1. Generate the still keyframe (Nanobanana)
steps.text: First make one strong static frame of the substance. Use Nanobanana at 16:9, 1K. Keep it perfectly centered on pure black with lots of negative space — that black background is what makes it loop and blend into any dark UI later.
steps.prompt: A single glossy liquid mercury blob made of glowing violet and magenta chrome, floating centered on a pure black background. Smooth reflective metallic surface with soft internal glow, organic rounded droplet shape with gentle surface tension, subtle bloom. Studio product look, high detail, centered composition, lots of black negative space around it. No text, no logo.

steps.title: 2. Animate it into a living loop (Veo 3)
steps.text: Feed that still into Veo 3 as the input image. Ask for slow, cyclical, in-place motion only — no camera movement, no cuts — so it can be looped seamlessly in the UI. Duration 6s (Veo 3 accepts 4-8s).
steps.prompt: The purple liquid mercury blob slowly morphs and flows in place, surface ripples with viscous liquid-metal waves, gentle undulation, highlight glides across the chrome surface, soft inner glow pulses. Static locked camera, blob stays centered, pure black background. Smooth continuous cyclical motion, no cuts, no text.

steps.title: 3. Drop it into your product
steps.text: Because the background is pure black and the motion is centered and cyclical, you can place the clip behind a headline with a <video autoplay muted loop playsinline> tag and mix-blend-mode: screen (or just on a dark section). It reads as a native, expensive-looking AI essence. Tip: keep the export compressed to ~5-10MB h264 1080p.

guides.result: A seamless, loopable purple liquid-metal AI orb you can autoplay behind any hero headline — generated in two steps: Nanobanana for the frame, Veo 3 for the motion.

guides.title: Optimize how you ship UI in Quadcode
guides.desc: The full playbook: brief → screen map → component system → states → polish. Cut rework and get production-ready interfaces faster.

steps.title: Lock the product brief in one pass
steps.text: Before any pixels, force a tight brief: who it's for, the one job of the screen, and what 'done' looks like. Ambiguous briefs are what burn tokens later.
steps.prompt: Act as a product designer inside Quadcode. Turn my rough idea into a locked UI brief with: (1) user + job-to-be-done, (2) primary screen goal in one sentence, (3) must-have vs nice-to-have, (4) platform (web/desktop/mobile) + density, (5) success criteria I can check visually. Ask at most 3 clarifying questions only if a blocker is missing — otherwise assume sensible defaults and proceed.

steps.title: Map screens before you generate
steps.text: List every screen and the navigation between them. Agents that jump straight to a pretty mock invent dead ends; a map keeps the system coherent.
steps.prompt: From the locked brief, produce a screen map: numbered screens, purpose of each, entry/exit, and the primary CTA. Output as a compact table + a one-line user flow. Flag any screen that can be merged or cut. Do not write visual styles yet.

steps.title: Define the component system once
steps.text: Tokens and components first — then every screen reuses them. This is the biggest speed win: change the system once instead of restyling ten mocks.
steps.prompt: Design a minimal UI system for this product: color tokens (bg/surface/text/accent/danger), type scale, spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24), radius, elevation, and a component inventory (Button, Input, Select, Card, Nav, Table/List, Modal, Toast, Empty state). Specify default / hover / focus / disabled for interactive parts. Keep it implementable in HTML/CSS or React without a design-tool roundtrip.

steps.title: Build the hero screen with real states
steps.text: Generate the main screen in three states: empty, loading, populated. Interfaces fail in the edges — bake them in on the first pass.
steps.prompt: Using the system above, implement the primary screen as production-ready UI (HTML/CSS or the project's stack). Include: empty state, loading skeleton, and populated data. Use realistic copy and sample data — no lorem. Match the tokens exactly. Prefer one composition, strong hierarchy, no decorative card clutter.

steps.title: Stress-test and ship the checklist
steps.text: Close with a QA pass the agent can run itself: contrast, keyboard focus, mobile width, reduced motion, and copy length. Then export the handoff notes.
steps.prompt: Run an interface QA pass on what we built. Check: contrast, focus rings, 375px layout, truncated labels, empty/error copy, reduced-motion. Fix anything broken. End with a short handoff: file list, tokens used, remaining TODOs, and the exact prompt I should reuse next time to extend this UI without drifting the system.

guides.result: A repeatable Quadcode UI workflow — brief, map, system, states, QA — so every new interface starts faster and ships cleaner.

guides.title: Build a SaaS analytics dashboard UI
guides.desc: One brief → dense but readable dashboard: KPI row, chart zone, activity table, and filters that feel native.

steps.title: Frame the dashboard job
steps.text: Name the operator and the decision they make in under 10 seconds. Dashboards without a decision become wallpaper.
steps.prompt: Design a dark SaaS analytics dashboard for a B2B ops lead who needs to spot revenue risk in under 10 seconds. Product: Northline. Sections: left nav, top bar with date range + workspace switcher, KPI strip (4 metrics), main chart, secondary breakdown, and a filterable activity table. Aesthetic: calm, dense, professional — not playful. Stack: single HTML/CSS/JS page.

steps.title: Lay out hierarchy and density
steps.text: KPIs first, chart second, table last. Keep filters sticky. Ask for realistic numbers so the layout is tested with real string lengths.
steps.prompt: Implement the full dashboard layout with realistic sample data (currency, % deltas, timestamps). KPI cards show value + delta + sparkline placeholder. Chart area uses a simple SVG line chart. Table has status pills, search, and column sort affordances. Use CSS grid; no card soup; one clear visual hierarchy.

steps.title: Add interaction without noise
steps.text: Date range, row hover, and empty filter results — enough to feel alive, not a motion demo.
steps.prompt: Add light interactions: date-range segmented control, table filter that updates visible rows, row hover, and an empty-filter state. Prefer CSS transitions under 200ms. Respect prefers-reduced-motion. No purple glow, no glassmorphism.

guides.result: A production-shaped SaaS dashboard UI you can hand to engineering or keep iterating inside Quadcode.

guides.title: Ship a mobile app screen kit
guides.desc: Onboarding, home, and detail — three linked mobile screens with shared components so the product feels like one app.

steps.title: Define the mobile product spine
steps.text: Three screens only for v1: onboarding, home, detail. Everything else waits.
steps.prompt: Plan a mobile consumer app UI kit (390×844). App: Pulse Habit. Screens: (1) onboarding with value prop + CTA, (2) home with today's habits + progress, (3) habit detail with streak and log action. Shared: bottom tab bar, primary button, list row. Light theme, soft neutrals, one accent. Output the screen map first.

steps.title: Generate a visual keyframe for mood
steps.text: One still locks color and photography mood before you code screens — optional but keeps the kit on-brand.
steps.prompt: Clean mobile UI mockup keyframe, habit-tracking home screen on a phone, soft neutral background, coral accent, large readable type, progress rings, calm lifestyle photo header, modern iOS-like spacing, centered product shot, no tiny unreadable text, 9:16

steps.title: Implement the three screens as one system
steps.text: Code them together so tokens and components stay shared. Link with simple in-page navigation.
steps.prompt: Implement all three Pulse Habit screens as one HTML/CSS file with shared tokens and components. Include a simple screen switcher. Use realistic copy. Touch targets ≥44px. Safe-area padding. Empty and completed habit states on home.

guides.result: A coherent three-screen mobile UI kit — onboarding, home, detail — ready to extend or hand off.

guides.title: Atlas Freight — Cinematic Logistics Landing
guides.desc: Build an awwwards-tier dark cinematic freight landing page with an AI-generated hero video, a scroll-driven truck parallax scene and a live route map, using a GPT-Image → Kling → Topaz pipeline.

steps.title: Generate the cinematic hero keyframe
steps.text: Use GPT-Image to create a photorealistic 16:9 aerial keyframe of a container seaport at golden hour. This still becomes the mobile poster and the source frame for the hero video.
steps.prompt: Cinematic aerial hero plate, sprawling container seaport at golden hour, stacked containers in rows, ship-to-shore gantry cranes, container ship at berth, atmospheric haze + low sun flare, warm amber raking light, teal-and-orange cinematic grade, high aerial drone view, strong leading lines, 24mm, deep DOF, photorealistic, film grain. No text/logos/people.

steps.title: Generate the night-loading interlude keyframe
steps.text: A second GPT-Image keyframe — a moody night loading scene — drives a mid-page cinematic interlude with multi-layer parallax.
steps.prompt: Cinematic night plate, containers loaded onto a cargo ship, gantry crane silhouettes vs deep blue-black sky, spreader lifting a container, floodlights + sodium lamps, harbor-light bokeh, industrial fog, moody teal-and-amber low-key grade, wide frame, crane silhouette foreground-left, dark negative space upper for overlay, 35mm, shallow DOF. No text/logos.

steps.title: Generate service card photography
steps.text: Four square GPT-Image stills — ocean, air, road & rail, warehouse — feed the services grid. Keep the same teal-and-amber grade and leave deep shadow area for text legibility.
steps.prompt: Low-angle photoreal container ship at sea, teal-and-amber cinematic grade, deep shadow area lower third for text overlay, no text/logos. (Repeat with matching grade for: cargo freighter loading at sunset / night truck with light trails beside a parallel train / automated high-bay warehouse with AGVs.)

steps.title: Animate the hero keyframe into video
steps.text: Feed the hero keyframe into Kling image-to-video (5s, pro) with slow, steady camera language — no cuts, no shake — so it loops seamlessly behind the fold.
steps.prompt: Very slow dolly-in / Ken Burns push, drifting haze, sun flare, water shimmer, distant crane trolley inches along, extremely smooth steady drone motion, 24mm, deep DOF, single continuous shot. Negative: text, watermark, fast motion, camera shake, cuts, distortion.

steps.title: Animate the interlude keyframe into video
steps.text: Same Kling pipeline on the night-loading keyframe, tuned for a slow lateral drift so it reads well under a 3-layer ScrollTrigger parallax.
steps.prompt: Spreader slowly lowers a container, slow lateral parallax drift, floodlights, twinkling harbor bokeh, rolling fog, rim light on steel, smooth slow motion, single shot.

steps.title: Upscale both clips
steps.text: Run both Kling clips through Topaz video_upscale (Proteus model, 2x, forced H.264) for crisp full-screen playback and guaranteed <video> compatibility.

steps.title: Generate the road-journey parallax assets
steps.text: For the scroll-driven 'trucks physically drive forward' scene, generate the truck and roadside layers with GPT-Image on a flat white background (transparent background isn't supported directly), then key out the white and auto-crop.
steps.prompt: Photoreal semi-truck with container, side profile facing right, graphite-blue metallic body with reflections, glowing headlight bloom, red taillights, amber brand stripe, real tire tread detail, dusk teal-and-amber grade matching a cinematic seaport scene, on a flat pure white background, no shadow, no text/logos.

steps.title: Wire it all together with GSAP ScrollTrigger
steps.text: Hand-code the page in vanilla HTML/CSS/JS with GSAP + ScrollTrigger + MotionPathPlugin (CDN, no build step): hero Ken-Burns scrub, 3-layer interlude parallax, SVG world-map route movers, scroll-scrubbed truck MotionPath with velocity-driven wheel spin/motion-blur/dust, animated counters, a fake-live tracker/dashboard via setInterval, and prefers-reduced-motion + mobile poster fallback throughout.

steps.title: Preview the finished site
steps.text: Here is the actual page in the browser — a real, scrollable single-page site. The AI clips now live inside it as the hero and interlude backgrounds, while GSAP drives the scroll-through: hero headline, the four-mode services grid, the scroll-driven road parallax scene, the live world route map + tracker, the fake-live dashboard, and the quote CTA. Scroll it top to bottom to see every section animate.

guides.result: A fully working, awwwards-tier cinematic logistics landing page — recorded here as a 12-second scroll-through of the real page in the browser. The AI-generated hero and interlude clips are wired in as section backgrounds, wrapped in a hand-coded GSAP site: a scroll-driven truck parallax scene with living motion (spinning wheels, motion blur, dust, dynamic headlight pool), a real animated world route map, a fake-live tracker and dashboard, and a quote CTA — all a single static page with zero backend.

guides.title: Boss fight: one brief, two AI models
guides.desc: Give the same boss-fight brief to two video models and cut the takes side by side — the split-screen format that starts arguments in the comments.

steps.title: Write one brief both models get
steps.text: The comparison only works if the brief is identical. Describe the fight beats — approach, dodge, counter — but leave the world open, so each model shows its own taste in setting and monster design.
steps.prompt: Cinematic dark-fantasy boss fight, game-cinematic quality: a lone armored hero faces a towering monstrous boss, the hero approaches weapon drawn, dodges a massive strike, and counters. Dynamic camera following the action, dramatic lighting, volumetric atmosphere, photoreal detail, 18 seconds

steps.title: Generate a take on each model
steps.text: Run the exact same prompt on both models. In this run one staged a plague abomination in a flooded gothic cathedral, the other a horned demon in a torch-lit colosseum — same beats, totally different directors.

steps.title: Cut the split-screen with labels
steps.text: Place the takes side by side, label each half with the model name, and let them run in sync. Viewers pick a winner themselves — that's the engagement hook. Play the clip above to see the result.

guides.result: An 18-second labeled split-screen where two AI models direct the same boss fight — a format built for 'left or right?' comment wars.

guides.title: Shoot a movie-grade mutation VFX scene
guides.desc: A syringe of glowing serum, a burst of pink energy, and a crystalline monster towering over fleeing scientists — one continuous lab-horror shot.

steps.title: Open on a macro detail
steps.text: Start tight and quiet: a needle piercing a specimen fills the frame. A macro close-up buys believability before the chaos — viewers lean in before the scene turns.
steps.prompt: Extreme macro close-up in a research lab: a syringe filled with glowing neon-pink serum slowly injects into a small yellow gelatinous specimen on a teal lab table, clinical lighting, shallow depth of field, cinematic film look

steps.title: Detonate the transformation
